turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the court this morning in case 23191, Williams v. Reed.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: This is a case about unemployment benefits in Alabama. The State of Alabama grants monetary benefits to unemployed claimants who meet certain criteria. To obtain those benefits, a claimant first must apply to the Alabama Department of Labor. In this case, 21 Alabama claimants applied for unemployment benefits, but they contend that the Alabama Department of Labor, in various ways, has unlawfully delayed the processing of their benefits claims. The 21 claimants therefore sued the Alabama Secretary of Labor in State Court, the Circuit Court of Montgomery County, Alabama. Invoking Section 1983 of Title 42, they asserted, among other things, that the department's delays in processing their benefits claims violated the Due Process Clause of the 14th Amendment and the Social Security Act of 1935. Importantly, the claimants did not ask the court to rule that they were entitled to unemployment benefits or to award them unemployment benefits. Rather, they simply asked the court to order the department to promptly address the benefits claims. Ultimately, the case went up to the Alabama Supreme Court, and that court ruled that the claimants first had to exhaust their administrative remedies under state law before bringing their Section 1983 lawsuit. The claimants then sought review of that ruling in this court, and we granted review, and we now reverse. In essence, Alabama has said that to challenge delays in the administrative process under Section 1983, you first have to exhaust the administrative process. Of course, that means that you can never challenge delays in the administrative process. That Catch-22 prevents the claimants here from obtaining a merits resolution of their 1983 claims in state court and, in effect, immunizes state officials from those kinds of 1983 suits for injunctive relief. Under this court's precedence, however, Alabama cannot maintain such an immunity rule.As this court's cases have repeatedly held, a state law that immunizes government conduct otherwise subject to suit under Section 1983 is preempted, even when this federal civil rights litigation takes place in state court. On that narrow ground, we reverse the judgment of the Alabama Supreme Court. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion, part two of which is joined by Justices Alito, Gorsuch, and Barrett.
